questions.q: Group cohomology $H^n(G,M)$ is defined as which derived functor?
choices: The left derived functors of $M\mapsto M_G$ (coinvariants)
The right derived functors of $M\mapsto M^G$ (invariants)
The right derived functors of the augmentation $\mathbb{Z}[G]\to\mathbb{Z}$
questions.hint: By definition $H^n(G,M)=R^n(-)^G(M)=\mathrm{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},M)$.
questions.explain: By definition $H^n(G,M)=R^n(-)^G(M)=\mathrm{Ext}^n_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},M)$. (Coinvariants give group homology $H_n(G,M)$.)

questions.q: On the bar resolution, an $n$-cochain $\varphi:G^n\to M$ has coboundary $(d\varphi)(g_1,\dots,g_{n+1})$ equal to which expression (degree 1 case)?
questions.hint: The bar formula in degree 1 gives $(d\varphi)(g_1,g_2)=g_1\varphi(g_2)-\varphi(g_1g_2)+\varphi(g_1)$.
questions.explain: The bar formula in degree 1 gives $(d\varphi)(g_1,g_2)=g_1\varphi(g_2)-\varphi(g_1g_2)+\varphi(g_1)$. A 1-cocycle (kernel of $d$) is exactly a crossed homomorphism.

questions.q: Which of the following are TRUE about $H^n(G,M)$?
choices: It depends on the choice of free resolution of $\mathbb{Z}$
It can be computed from any projective $\mathbb{Z}[G]$-resolution of $\mathbb{Z}$
$H^n(G,M)$ is naturally functorial in both $G$ and $M$
questions.hint: Cohomology is independent of the chosen projective resolution (the whole point of derived functors).
questions.explain: Cohomology is independent of the chosen projective resolution (the whole point of derived functors). The Ext-formula is the standard equivalent description, and functoriality in $G$ is what makes restriction, inflation, and corestriction work.

questions.q: Let $G=C_4$ act on $M=\mathbb{Z}/12$ via $\sigma\cdot x = 5x$. Compute $|H^0(G,M)|=|M^G|$.
questions.explain: $M^G=\ker(\sigma-1)=\{x:4x\equiv 0\bmod 12\}=\{0,3,6,9\}$, so $|M^G|=\gcd(4,12)=4$. (Equivalently $|M^G|=\gcd(k-1,m)$ for $\sigma\cdot x=kx$ on $\mathbb{Z}/m$.)

questions.q: Why does the long exact sequence of cohomology even start? In other words, why does $0\to A^G\to B^G\to C^G$ NOT continue to a $\to 0$ for a short exact sequence $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$?
choices: Because $(-)^G$ is left exact but not right exact: a fixed element of $C$ may have no fixed-point lift in $B$
Because the connecting map $\delta$ is not well-defined
Because $A$ might fail to be normal in $B$
questions.hint: $(-)^G=\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},-)$ is left exact but not right exact.
questions.explain: $(-)^G=\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}[G]}(\mathbb{Z},-)$ is left exact but not right exact. The obstruction to lifting a fixed point in $C$ to a fixed point in $B$ lives in $H^1(G,A)$ — that is exactly $\delta(c)$.

questions.q: If $G$ acts trivially on $M$, what is $H^0(G,M)$?
questions.hint: Trivial action means every element is fixed: $M^G=M$.
questions.explain: Trivial action means every element is fixed: $M^G=M$. (Note: $\mathrm{Hom}(G,M)$ is what $H^1$ becomes under trivial action.)

questions.q: A crossed homomorphism $\varphi:G\to M$ satisfies which identity?
questions.hint: The cocycle condition $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\cdot\varphi(h)$ — this collapses to the homomorphism law when the action is trivial.
questions.explain: The cocycle condition $\varphi(gh)=\varphi(g)+g\cdot\varphi(h)$ — this collapses to the homomorphism law when the action is trivial.

questions.q: Hilbert's Theorem 90 states which of the following for a cyclic Galois extension $L/K$ with $G=\langle\sigma\rangle$?
choices: $H^1(G,L^\times)=0$: every $\beta\in L^\times$ with $N_{L/K}(\beta)=1$ has the form $\beta=\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$
$H^1(G,L)=0$: every trace-zero element is a coboundary
questions.hint: Hilbert 90 in cyclic form: $H^1(G,L^\times)=1$, equivalently norm-one elements are coboundaries $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$.
questions.explain: Hilbert 90 in cyclic form: $H^1(G,L^\times)=1$, equivalently norm-one elements are coboundaries $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha$. The fact about $L$ (additive) is the additive Hilbert 90, and the $H^2$ statement is the definition of relative Brauer.

questions.q: For $L/K=\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ and $\beta=\frac{3+4i}{5}$ (norm 1), Hilbert 90 produces $\alpha\in L^\times$ with $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha=\beta$. Which witness works?
questions.hint: Pythagorean trick: $\alpha=1+\beta$ works whenever it is nonzero, since $\sigma(1+\beta)=1+\bar\beta=1+\beta^{-1}=(1+\beta)/\beta\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\beta=\beta^{-1}(1+\beta)$, hence…
questions.explain: Pythagorean trick: $\alpha=1+\beta$ works whenever it is nonzero, since $\sigma(1+\beta)=1+\bar\beta=1+\beta^{-1}=(1+\beta)/\beta\cdot\beta^{-1}\cdot\beta=\beta^{-1}(1+\beta)$, hence $\sigma(\alpha)/\alpha=\beta^{-1}\cdot 1=\beta$? Carefully: $\sigma(1+\beta)/(1+\beta)=(1+\beta^{-1})/(1+\beta)=\beta^{-1}$, so adjust to $\alpha=\beta(1+\beta)$ if you want $\beta$ rather than $\beta^{-1}$ — the widget shows the explicit calculation.

questions.q: Equivalence classes of group extensions $1\to A\to E\to G\to 1$ (with prescribed $G$-action on abelian $A$) are classified by:
questions.hint: The cocycle $c(g,h)$ measuring failure of a section $s:G\to E$ to be a homomorphism is a 2-cocycle, and changing the section by $a:G\to A$ shifts $c$ by $da$.
questions.explain: The cocycle $c(g,h)$ measuring failure of a section $s:G\to E$ to be a homomorphism is a 2-cocycle, and changing the section by $a:G\to A$ shifts $c$ by $da$. The class $[c]\in H^2(G,A)$ classifies the extension.

questions.q: Which extensions correspond to the zero class $0\in H^2(G,A)$?
questions.hint: $[c]=0$ iff $c$ is a coboundary, iff some section $s$ exists with $c=0$, iff $s$ is a homomorphism, iff the extension splits.
questions.explain: $[c]=0$ iff $c$ is a coboundary, iff some section $s$ exists with $c=0$, iff $s$ is a homomorphism, iff the extension splits.

questions.q: Compute $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)$ (trivial action). What does each class correspond to?
questions.hint: By the periodic resolution, $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\mathbb{Z}/2$.
questions.explain: By the periodic resolution, $H^2(C_2,\mathbb{Z}/2)=\mathbb{Z}/2$. The trivial class is $V_4$ (split); the nontrivial class lifts $\sigma\in C_2$ to an order-4 element, giving $C_4$.

questions.q: For $G=C_n$ cyclic with generator $\sigma$ and $G$-module $M$, the cohomology in even and odd positive degrees is:
questions.hint: The two-step periodic resolution alternates $\sigma-1$ and the norm $N=1+\sigma+\dots+\sigma^{n-1}$.
questions.explain: The two-step periodic resolution alternates $\sigma-1$ and the norm $N=1+\sigma+\dots+\sigma^{n-1}$. Reading off the cochain complex: even degrees give $\ker(\sigma-1)/\mathrm{im}(N)=M^G/N(M)$, odd degrees give $\ker(N)/\mathrm{im}(\sigma-1)$.

questions.q: For $G=C_3$ acting trivially on $M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, compute $|H^2(C_3,\mathbb{Z}/6)|$.
questions.hint: Trivial action: $\sigma-1=0$ so $M^G=M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, and $N=1+1+1=3$ so $N(M)=3\cdot\mathbb{Z}/6=\{0,3\}$.
questions.explain: Trivial action: $\sigma-1=0$ so $M^G=M=\mathbb{Z}/6$, and $N=1+1+1=3$ so $N(M)=3\cdot\mathbb{Z}/6=\{0,3\}$. Then $H^2=M^G/N(M)=(\mathbb{Z}/6)/(2$-element subgroup$)=\mathbb{Z}/3$, of order 3.

questions.q: The Herbrand quotient $h(M)=|\hat H^0(G,M)|/|\hat H^{-1}(G,M)|$ has which key property?
choices: It is multiplicative on short exact sequences and trivial on finite modules
It equals the Euler characteristic of $M$
questions.hint: Herbrand quotient is multiplicative: $h(B)=h(A)h(C)$ for SES $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$, and $h(M)=1$ when $M$ is finite.
questions.explain: Herbrand quotient is multiplicative: $h(B)=h(A)h(C)$ for SES $0\to A\to B\to C\to 0$, and $h(M)=1$ when $M$ is finite. These two properties are what make it a usable invariant in class field theory.

questions.q: For $H\trianglelefteq G$ and a $G$-module $M$, the inflation–restriction five-term exact sequence reads:
questions.hint: This is the low-degree edge of the LHS spectral sequence.
questions.explain: This is the low-degree edge of the LHS spectral sequence. The crucial fixed-points $M^H$ in the inflation entry, and the $G/H$-invariants on the restriction entry, are what make the sequence work — both come from how the spectral sequence's $E_2$-page is built.

questions.q: The Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre spectral sequence has $E_2$-page given by:
questions.hint: Take cohomology of the normal subgroup first, then of the quotient.
questions.explain: Take cohomology of the normal subgroup first, then of the quotient. Note the order: $H$ goes inside (gives row $q$), and $G/H$ goes outside (gives column $p$).

questions.q: When does the LHS spectral sequence collapse on the row $q=0$ (so $H^p(G,M)=H^p(G/H,M^H)$ in low degrees)?
choices: When $|H|$ is invertible in the coefficient module $M$ (e.g., $|H|$ is a unit in a field of coefficients)
questions.hint: If $|H|$ is invertible in $M$, then $H^q(H,M)=0$ for $q\ge 1$ (cohomology of a finite group with coefficients in a divisible module vanishes).
questions.explain: If $|H|$ is invertible in $M$, then $H^q(H,M)=0$ for $q\ge 1$ (cohomology of a finite group with coefficients in a divisible module vanishes). The page is concentrated on row $q=0$, no differentials are possible, so $H^p(G,M)\cong H^p(G/H,M^H)$.

galois-cohomology-brauer.title: Galois cohomology and the Brauer group

questions.q: For a field $k$ with absolute Galois group $G_k=\mathrm{Gal}(\bar k/k)$, the Brauer group is defined as:
questions.hint: $\mathrm{Br}(k):=H^2(G_k,\bar k^\times)$.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Br}(k):=H^2(G_k,\bar k^\times)$. (Hilbert 90 says $H^1=0$, so the first nontrivial cohomology of $\bar k^\times$ is degree 2.) This $H^2$ classifies central simple $k$-algebras up to Morita equivalence.

questions.q: The Kummer exact sequence (with $n$ invertible in $k$) is $1\to\mu_n\to\bar k^\times\xrightarrow{x\mapsto x^n}\bar k^\times\to 1$. What does it imply about $H^1(k,\mu_n)$?
questions.hint: Long exact sequence: $k^\times\xrightarrow{n}k^\times\to H^1(k,\mu_n)\to H^1(k,\bar k^\times)=0$ by Hilbert 90.
questions.explain: Long exact sequence: $k^\times\xrightarrow{n}k^\times\to H^1(k,\mu_n)\to H^1(k,\bar k^\times)=0$ by Hilbert 90. So $H^1(k,\mu_n)=k^\times/(k^\times)^n$ — the Kummer isomorphism, foundation of Kummer theory.

questions.q: Which Brauer group is correct?
questions.hint: $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$ with generator $\mathbb{H}$.
questions.explain: $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{R})=\mathbb{Z}/2$ with generator $\mathbb{H}$. (For comparison: $\mathrm{Br}(\bar k)=0$, $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{F}_q)=0$ by Wedderburn, $\mathrm{Br}(\mathbb{Q}_p)=\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z}$ via the local invariant.)
